text:
1. Assume that the country of Rankinland is currently in recession.
(a) Assume that Rankinland produces only food and clot hing. Draw a correctly labeled production possibilities
curve for Rankinland. Show a point that could represent the current output combination and label it A.
(b) Assume that the Central Bank of Rankinla nd pursues an expansionary monetary policy.
(i) Identify the open-market operation that the Central Bank would use.
(ii) Draw a correctly labeled m oney market graph and show the short-run effect of the expansionary
monetary policy on the nominal interest rate.
(iii) Assuming no change to the price level, what ha ppens to the real interest rate as a result of the
expansionary monetary policy? Explain.
(iv) Given your answer to part (b)(iii) regarding the real interest rate, what happens to the real gross
domestic product (GDP) in the short run? Explain.
(c) Suppose Rankinland has a current account defic it. Rankinland’s currency is called the bera.
(i) What will initially happen to the current account deficit in Rankinland solely due to the change in the
real GDP from part (b)(iv) ? Explain.
(ii) What will happen to the international value of th e bera solely due to the change in the real GDP from
part (b)(iv) ? Explain.

points.description: a correctly labeled graph of the production possibilities curve (PPC).
showing point A inside the PPC.
identifying the buying of bonds as the correct open-market operation to use.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the money market.
showing a rightward shift of the money supply curve, resulting in a lower interest rate.
stating that the real interest rate will fall.
explaining that with the price level remaining constant, when the nominal interest rate falls, the real interest rate also falls.
stating that the real GDP will increase in the short run and explaining that investment or consumption increases, causing aggregate demand to increase.
stating that the current account deficit will increase.
explaining that the increase in real GDP increases income, which causes imports to increase and net exports to decrease.
stating that the international value of the bera will decrease.
explaining that the decline in the international value of the bera is due to an increase in the supply of the bera.

text:
2. The following is a simplified balance sh eet for Mi Tierra Bank in the United States.
(a) What is the reserve requirement?
(b) Assume that Luis withdraws $5,000 in cash from his checking account at Mi Tierra Bank.
(i) By how much will Mi Tierra Bank’s reserves change based on Luis’ withdrawal?
(ii) What is the initial effect of the withdrawal on the M1 measure of money supply? Explain.
(iii) As a result of the withdrawal, what is the ne w value of excess reserves on the balance sheet of
Mi Tierra Bank based on the reserve requirement from part (a) ?
(c) Assume that the next day John withdraws from Mi Tierra Bank an amount that exceeds the bank’s excess
reserves. Assuming that no loans are called in, how can Mi Tierra Bank cover its required reserves?

points.description: calculating the correct reserve requirement of 10 percent ($10,000/$100,000).
stating that total bank reserves will decrease by $5,000.
stating that the $5,000 withdrawal has no effect on the M1 measure of the money supply because it only changes the composition of M1 between cash and demand deposits.
stating that the new value of the excess reserves is $500.
stating that the bank can borrow from the Federal Reserve or from another bank.

text:
3. Assume the economy of Andersonland is in a long-run equilibrium with full employment. In the short run,
nominal wages are fixed.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of short-run aggregate supply, long-run aggregate supply, and aggregate
demand. Show each of the following.
(i) Equilibrium output, labeled Y 1
(ii) Equilibrium price level, labeled PL 1
(b) Assume that there is an increase in exports from Andersonland. On your graph in part (a), show the effect of
higher exports on the equilibrium in the short run, labeling the new equilibrium output and price level Y2
and PL2, respectively.
(c) Based on your answer in part (b), what is the imp act of higher exports on real wages in the short run?
Explain.
(d) As a result of the increase in exports, export-oriented industries in Andersonland increase expenditures on
new container ships and equipment.
(i) What component of aggregate demand will change?
(ii) What is the impact on the long-run aggregate supply? Explain

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph and showing the AD and SRAS curves and PL1.
showing a vertical LRAS curve at the output Y1 through the intersection of the SRAS and AD curves.
showing a rightward shift of the aggregate demand curve and showing Y2 and PL2.
stating that real wages will fall because the price level has increased and the nominal wages are fixed in the short run.
stating that the investment component of AD will change.
stating that the long-run aggregate supply curve will shift to the right because the capital stock has increased. GO ON TO THE NEXT PAGE.

text:
1. Assume that the economy of country Alpha is operating at the full-employment equilibrium.
(a) Using a correctly labeled graph of aggregate dema nd, short-run aggregate supply, and long-run aggregate
supply, show the equilibrium, labeling the equilibrium price level as PL1 and the equilibrium output as Y1.
(b) Now assume that the economy of country Beta, a major trading partner of Alpha, enters a recession. On your
graph from part (a), show the effect of country Beta’s recession on each of the following in country Alpha in
the short run.
(i) Aggregate demand. Explain.
(ii) Real output and price level, labeled as Y2 and PL2
(c) The central bank of Alpha wishes to offset the eff ect of Beta’s recession on Alpha’s real output. What open-
market operation should the central bank of Alpha undertake?
(d) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the money market for Alpha, and show the effect of the open-market
operation you identified in part (c) on the nominal interest rate in the short run.
(e) Assume now that the real interest rate in Beta beco mes greater than the real interest rate in Alpha. Based on
the change in real interest rate, answer each of the following.
(i) Will financial capital flow from Alpha to Beta or from Beta to Alpha?
(ii)
Will the value of Alpha’s currency on the foreign exchange market relative to Beta’s currency
appreciate, stay the same, or depreciate? Explain.

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph, AD and SRAS, and showing P L sub 1
drawing a vertical LRAS at Y sub 1
showing a leftward shift of the AD curve and showing a lower real output, Y sub 2 and price level, P L sub 2
explaining that the recession in Beta decreases the demand for Alpha’s products, and therefore Alpha’s exports to Beta will decrease.

stating that the central bank should buy bonds.The figure shows a graph with a horizontal axis labeled Quantity of Money and a vertical axis labeled Nominal Interest Rate. Two quantities appear on the horizontal axis and are labeled, from left to right, M sub 1 and M sub 2. Two rates appear on the vertical axis and are labeled, from bottom to top, r sub 2 and r sub 1. A vertical, downward-facing arrow points from r sub 1 to r sub 2. Two vertical lines run parallel to the vertical axis and are labeled, from left to right, M S sub1 and M S sub 2. Line M S sub 1 begins on the horizontal axis at quantity M sub 1 and moves upward. Line M S sub 2 begins on the horizontal axis at quantity M sub 2 and moves upward. A horizontal, right-facing arrow points from line M S sub 1 to line M S sub 2. A downward-sloping curve begins above r sub 1 and to the right of the vertical axis, and is labeled M D. Curve M D moves downward and to the right, passing through Line M S sub 1 at the coordinates M sub 1 and r sub 1, continues down and to the right, intersects line M S sub 2 at the coordinates M sub 2 and r sub 2, and ends below r sub 2 and above the horizontal axis. Curve M D does not intersect the horizontal or vertical axis
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the money market.
showing a rightward shift of the money supply curve leading to a lower interest rate.
stating that financial capital will flow from Alpha to Beta.

stating that the value of Alpha’s currency will depreciate and for explaining that higher interest rate in Beta will increase the demand for its financial assets, increasing the supply of Alpha’s currency (or decreasing the demand for Alpha’s currency).

text:
2. The simplified T-account (balance sh eet) for Zenith Bank in the country of Everland is shown below. Assume
the reserve requirement in Everland is 20 percent.
(a) Calculate the maximum amount by which Zenith Bank can increase its loans.
(b) Based on your answer in part (a), calculate the maximum possible increase in Everland’s money supply.
(c) Identify one reason that Everland’s money supply may expand by a smaller amount than the amount you
identified in part (b).
(d) Assume the central bank of Everland purchases $10 million in government securities from the public. If the
public deposits the $10 million in Zenith Bank, by how much will Zenith Bank’s required reserves change as
a result of the new deposit?
(e) What will happen to the price of bonds as a result of the central bank’s purchase of government securities
described in part (d) ?

points.description: calculating the maximum loan: Maximum loan amount equals Excess reserves equals 80 dollars minus, open parenthesis zero point 2 times 2 hundred dollars close parenthesis, equals forty million dollars.

calculating the maximum increase in the money supply (MS): Range in the M S equals open parenthesis 1 divided by point 2 zero close parenthesis, times, open parenthesis forty dollars close parenthesis, equals 2 hundred million dollars.
stating that either the public may not redeposit all the money back in the account (cash leakages) or the bank may choose to hold additional reserves.
stating that the bank’s required reserves will increase by $2 million.
stating that the price of bonds will increase.

text:
3. Unemployment is an economic probl em that a nation frequently faces.
(a) Using the data from the table above, calculate the unemployment rate, as a percentage, for Country X.
(b) Assume that the natural rate of une mplo yment for Country X is 6 percent.
(i) Draw a single correctly labeled graph and s how both the short-run Phillips curve and the long-run
Phillips curve.
(ii) Using the unemployment rate you calculated in part (a) and assuming an actual inflation rate of
2 percent, show the current combination of unemployment and inflation on your graph in part (b)(i),
and label it as Z.
(c) In the absence of any policy action, will the short-r un aggregate supply for Country X increase, decrease, or
remain constant in the long run? Explain.
(d) If the actual unemployment rate equals the natural rate of 6 percent, is there cyclical unemployment in
Country X?

points.description: calculating the unemployment rate(UR): U R equals Number of unemployed divided by Labor force, equals 80 divided by 800, equals 10 percent. The figure shows a graph with a horizontal axis labeled Unemployment Rate, in percent, and a vertical axis labeled Inflation Rate, in percent. Two rates appear on the horizontal axis and are labeled, from left to right, 6 and 10. One rate appears on the vertical axis, approximately one-third of the way up the vertical axis, and is labeled 2. There are two lines on the graph. The first line is a downward sloping line labeled S R P C. The line starts near the vertical axis and moves down and to the right, and ends near the horizontal axis. The second line is a vertical line labeled L R P C, and begins on the horizontal axis at 6 Percent Unemployment Rate. Line L R P C intersects line S R P C at a point near the middle of each line. There is a point labeled Z on line S R P C, to the right of line L R P C, at the coordinates for 10 Percent Unemployment Rate and 2 Percent Inflation Rate.
correctly labeled graph of the Phillips curve, a downward-sloping short-run, and a vertical long-run Phillips curve
showing point Z on the short-run Phillips curve at the coordinates Open parenthesis, 10 comma 2, close parenthesis
stating that the SRAS curve will shift to the right and for explaining that input costs will fall in the long run. (
stating that there is no cyclical unemployment.

text:
1. Assume that a country’s economy is in long-run equilibrium.
(a) Using a correctly labeled graph of aggregate dema nd, short-run aggregate supply, and long-run aggregate
supply, show the short-run equilibrium price level, labeled PL1, and output level, labeled Y1.
(b) Assume that increased uncertainty has reduced business orders for equipment. What is the impact of the
change in business orders on each of the following in the short run?
(i) Aggregate demand. Explain.
(ii) Employment
(c) Based on the change in business orders, what will happen to the long-run economic growth rate? Explain.
(d) Using a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market, show the effect of the change in business
orders on the real interest rate in the country in the short run.
(e) Given the effect on the real interest rate identified in part (d), what will happen to each of the following?
(i) The supply of the country’s currency on the foreign exchange market. Explain.
(ii) The international value of the country’s currency

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph, showing A D, S R A S, and P L sub one.
drawing a vertical • L R A S curve at Y sub one that is at the intersection of A D and S R A S.
concluding that • A D decreases because investment is a component of A D.
concluding that employment decreases.
stating that long-run economic growth rate will decrease.

explaining that lower investment will decrease capital formation. • • •The figure shows a graph with a horizontal axis labeled Real G D P, and a vertical axis labeled Price Level. One point appears on the horizontal axis and is labeled Y sub one. One point appears on the vertical axis and is labeled P L sub one. There are three lines on the graph. A vertical line labeled L R A S runs parallel to the vertical axis. Line L R A S begins on the horizontal axis at point Y sub one and moves upward. A downward sloping line begins above P L sub one, to the right of the vertical axis, and to the left of line L R A S, and is labeled A D. Line A D, moves down and to the right, passes through line L R A S at the coordinates Y sub one and P L sub one, and ends above the horizontal axis. An upward sloping line begins below P L sub one, to the right of the vertical axis, and to the left of line L R A S, and is labeled S R A S. Line S R A S moves up and to the right, passes through line L R A S at the coordinates Y sub one and P L sub one, and ends above line A D.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market.
shifting the demand curve to the left and showing a decrease in the real interest rate.
stating that the supply of the country’s currency will increase.
explaining that because of the lower real interest rate within the country, domestic funds will be attracted to financial assets in foreign countries in order to receive higher returns.

stating that the country’s currency will depreciate in value.The figure shows a graph with a horizontal axis labeled Quantity of Loanable Funds, and a vertical axis labeled Real Interest Rate. Two quantities appear on the horizontal axis and are labeled, from left to right, Q prime and Q. Two rates appear on the vertical axis and are labeled, from bottom to top, r prime and r. A vertical, downward-facing arrow points from r to r prime. There are three lines on the graph. Two downward sloping lines begin to the right of the vertical axis, and move down and to the right, and end before reaching the horizontal axis. The upper downward sloping line is labeled D, and the lower downward sloping line is labeled D prime. An arrow points, down and to the left, from line D to line D prime. An upward sloping line begins to the left of Q prime and belo

text:
2. Kathy deposits $10,000 cash into her checking account at First Federal Bank. The required reserve ratio is
20 percent.
(a) Calculate the maximum possible change in First Federal’s loans as a result of Kathy’s deposit.
(b) After all adjustments in the banking system have occurred, calculate the maximum possible change in each
of the following as a result of Kathy’s deposit.
(i) Demand deposits in the banking system
(ii) The money supply
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the money market and show the effect of Kathy’s deposit on the nominal
interest rate.
(d) Identify ONE reason that the change in the money supply could differ from the value calculated in
part (b)(ii).

points.description: calculating $8,000.
calculating the maximum possible change in demand deposits: $50,000.
calculating the maximum possible change in money supply: $40,000.
drawing a correctly labeled money market graph.
shifting the supply curve to the right and showing a decrease in the nominal interest rate.

identifying a correct reason, e.g. banks holding excess reserves, cash leakage, etc. • • • • • •The figure shows a graph with a horizontal axis labeled Quantity of Money and a vertical axis labeled Nominal Interest Rate. Two quantities appear on the horizontal axis and are labeled, from left to right, M sub 1 and M sub 2. Two rates appear on the vertical axis and are labeled, from bottom to top, r sub 2 and r sub 1. A vertical, downward-facing arrow points from r sub 1 to r sub 2. Two vertical lines run parallel to the vertical axis and are labeled, from left to right, M S sub1 and M S sub 2. Line M S sub 1 begins on the horizontal axis at quantity M sub 1 and moves upward. Line M S sub 2 begins on the horizontal axis at quantity M sub 2 and moves upward. A horizontal, right-facing arrow points from line M S sub 1 to line M S sub 2. A downward-sloping curve begins above r sub 1 and to the right of the vertical axis, and is labeled M D. Curve M D moves downward and to the right, passing through Line M S sub 1 at the coordinates M sub 1 and r sub 1, continues down and to the right, intersects line M S sub 2 at the coordinates M sub 2 and r sub 2, and ends below r sub 2 and above the horizontal axis. Curve M D does not intersect the horizontal or vertical axes.

text:
3. The economy in Country X is in a recession, with real gross domestic product (GDP) $100 billion below
full-employment output.
(a) Draw one correctly labeled graph of the short -run and long-run Phillips curves, labeling the current
equilibrium point A.
(b) Assume that the government increases spending by $ 20 billion to stimulate economic activity. Assume that
the marginal propensity to save is 0.25. Calculate the maximum total change in real GDP that could occur
following the $20 billion increase in government spending.
(c) On your graph in part (a), label the new equilibri um point B as a result of the increase in government
spending.
(d) Had the government lowered pers onal income taxes by $20 billion instead of increasing spending by
$20 billion, would the maximum total change in real GDP be greater than, smaller than, or the same as
the one calculated in part (b) ? Explain.

points.description: correctly labeled graph of the Phillips curve, with a downward-sloping short-run and a vertical long-run Phillips curve
showing point A that is on the short-run Phillips curve to the right of the long-run Phillips curve.
calculating the maximum possible change in real G D P: $80 billion.
placing point B on the S R P C to the left of point A.
showing point B on the short-run Phillips curve between point A and the long-run Phillips curve.

stating that the change caused by tax decrease would be smaller because: part of the increase in personal income will be saved rather than consumed, or students may show by calculating the increase in • • - - G D P, $60 billion, or because the tax multiplier is smaller than the spending multiplier -The figure shows a graph with a horizontal axis labeled Unemployment Rate, in percent, and a vertical axis labeled Inflation Rate, in percent. There are two lines on the graph. The first line is a downward sloping line labeled S R P C. The line starts near the vertical axis, moves down and to the right, and ends above the horizontal axis. The second line is a vertical line labeled L R P C. The line begins on the horizontal axis and intersects line S R P C at a point near the middle of each line. There is a point labeled B on line S R P C, to the right of line L R P C, and there is a point labeled A on line S R P C, to the right of point B, with dashed lines connecting it vertically and horizontally to the axes.

text:
1. The economy of Zarland is operating below the fu ll-employment level of output with a balanced budget.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of short- run aggregate supply, long-run aggregate supply, and aggregate
demand, and show each of the following.
(i) The country’s current equilibriu m outp ut and price level, labeled Y1 and PL1, res pectively
(ii) The full-employment output, labeled Y f
(b) If Zarland increases government expenditures and taxes by equal amounts, can ag gregate demand increase?
Explain.
(c) If Zarland decides to pursue an expansionary m onetary policy, what open-market operation should the
central bank undertake?
(d) Using a correctly labeled graph of the money market, show the short-run effect of the open-market operation
you identified in part (c) on the interest rate.
(e) As a result of the interest rate change associated with the expansionary monetary policy, will the supply of
Zarland’s currency in the foreign exchange market increase, decrease, or remain the same in the short run?
Explain.
(f) Based on your answer in part (e), will the value of Zarland’s currency in the foreign exchange market
increase, decrease, or remain the same in the short run?
(g) Following the change in the value of Zarland’s currency that you identified in part (f), will Zarland’s exports
increase, decrease, or remain the same in the short run? Explain. . . .. . .

If Zarland increases government expenditures and taxes by equal amounts, can aggregate demand increase?

text:
2. Assume that the current unemployment rate in Count ry A is lower than the natural rate of unemployment.
(a) Draw a single correctly labeled graph with both the long-run Philli ps curve and the short-run Phillips curve.
Label the current short-run equilibrium point Z.
(b) Identify a specific fiscal policy action that would bring the economy to full employment.
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market, and show the effect of the fiscal policy from
part (b) on the real interest rate in the short run.
(d) Now assume instead that there is no fiscal policy acti on. Will the short-run Phillips curve shift to the right,
shift to the left, or remain the same over time? Explain

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph showing a vertical L R P C and a downward sloping S R P C.
showing point Z on the S R P C to the left of L R P C.
stating that a contractionary fiscal policy such as an increase in taxes or a decrease in government expenditure will return the economy to full employment.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market.
shifting the supply of loanable funds curve to the right or the demand for loanable funds to the left and showing a decrease in the real interest rate.

stating that S R P C will shift to the right (upwards) and for explaining that inflationary expectations increase, or input prices increase, or wages increase. Two quantities appear on the horizontal axis and are labeled, from left to right, Q prime and Q. Two rates appear on the vertical axis and are labeled, from bottom to top, r prime and r. A vertical, downward facing arrow points from r to r prime. There are three lines on the graph. Two parallel downward sloping lines begin to the left of the vertical axis, and move down and to the right, and end before reaching the horizontal axis. The upper downward sloping line is labeled D, and the lower downward sloping line is labeled D prime. An arrow points, down and to the left, from line D to line D prime. An upward sloping line begins to the left of Q prime and below r prime, and is labeled S. Line S moves up and to the right, and passes through line D prime at the coordinates Q prime and r prime, continues up and to the right, and passes through line D at the coordinates Q and r. Or The second graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Quantity of Loanable Funds and the vertical axis labeled Real Interest Rate. There are two points on the horizontal axis labeled, from left to right, as Q and Q prime. There are two points on the vertical axis labeled, from top to bottom, as r and r prime. There are three lines on the graph. There is a line labeled D that begins to the right of the vertical axis and above point r and slopes downward from left to right. There are two parallel lines labeled S and S prime, which slope upward from left to right. Line S is to the left of line S prime, and intersects line D at a point with coordinates Q and r. Line S prime intersects line D at a point with coordinates Q prime and r prime. There is a vertical arrow pointing downward from r to r prime, a horizontal arrow pointing from Q to Q prime, and a horizontal arrow pointing from S to S prime.

text:
3. The Bank of Uchenna has the following balance sheet.
Assume that the reserve requirement ratio is 20 percent.
(a) Calculate the dollar value of the reserves that the Bank of Uchenna is required to hold.
(b) Given the current reserves, calculate the maximum value of additional loans that the Bank of Uchenna
can make.
(c) Assume that Elike raises $5,000 in cash from a yard sale and deposits the cash in his checking account at the
Bank of Uchenna. By how much does the money supply immediately change as a result of Elike’s deposit?
(d) Calculate the maximum change in demand deposits in the banking system as a whole resulting from Elike’s
deposit.
(e) If the Bank of Uchenna is not mee ting its reserve requirement, what action can it take to meet the reserve
requirement without calling in loans or selling property?

points.description: calculating the required reserves: $20,000.
calculating the maximum amount of additional loans that Bank of Uchenna can make as 10,000 dollars, open parenthesis, equals 30,000 dollars minus 20,000 dollars, close parenthesis. .
stating that there will be no change in the money supply.
stating that the maximum change in demand deposits in the banking system is 25,000 dollars, open parenthesis, equals 5,000 dollars times 5, close parenthesis. .
stating that Bank of Uchenna can borrow from other banks to meet its reserve requirements or borrow from the central bank.

text:
1. Assume that a country’s economy is in a short-run equilibrium and the actual unemployment rate is lower than
the natural rate of unemployment.
(a) Using a correctly labeled graph of the long-run aggregate supply curve, short-run aggregate supply curve,
and aggregate demand curve, show each
of the following.
(i) Current price level, labeled PL 1, and current output level, labeled
(b) What open-market operation can the country’s central bank use to move the economy toward its long-run
equilibrium?
(c) Use a correctly labeled money-market graph to s how how the country’s central bank action to move the
economy toward its long-run equilibrium affects the equilibrium nominal interest rate in the short run.
(d) Based on the interest rate change from part (c), will each of the following increase, decrease, or remain the
same in the short run?
(i) Real output. Explain.
(e) Assume instead that the central bank does not pursue the monetary policy action from part (b) and there was
no other government intervention. Will each of the following increase, decrease, or remain the same in the
long run?
(i) Short-run aggregate supply. Explain.
(ii) Employment . . . . ..
1. Assume that a country’s economy is in a short-run eq uilibrium and the actual unemployment rate is lower than
the natural rate of unemployment.
(a) Using a correctly labeled graph of the long-run aggregate supply curve, short-run aggregate supply curve,
and aggregate demand curve, show each of the following.
(i) Current price level, labeled PL 1, and current output level, labeled Y1
(ii) The full-employment output level, labeled Y F
(b) What open-market operation can the country’s central bank use to move the economy toward its long-run
equilibrium?
(c) Use a correctly labeled money-market graph to s how how the country’s central bank action to move the
economy toward its long-run equilibrium affects the equilibrium nominal interest rate in the short run.
(d) Based on the interest rate change from part (c), will each of the following increase, decrease, or remain the
same in the short run?
(i) Real output. Explain.
(ii) Natural rate of unemployment

no other government intervention. Will each of the following increase, decrease, or remain the same in the
long run?
(i) Short-run aggregate supply. Explain.
(ii) Employment

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph showing a downward-sloping A D curve, an upward-sloping S R A S curve, equilibrium price level, P L 1, and output level,Y 1. 
showing a vertical L R A S curve at Y F to the left of the equilibrium output level.
stating that the central bank can sell bonds.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the money market. 
showing a leftward shift in the money supply curve, resulting in a higher nominal interest rate.
stating that real output will decrease and for explaining that interest-sensitive spending will decrease, resulting in a decrease in aggregate demand and real output. 
stating that the natural rate of unemployment will remain unchanged.
stating that the short-run aggregate supply open parenthesis, S R A S, close parenthesis. curve will shift to the left in the long run and for explaining that nominal wages will increase. 
stating that employment will decrease.

text:
2. The following is the balance sheet for Raymond Bank, one of many commercial banks in a country.
Assume a 10 percent reserve requirement.
(a) Calculate Raymond Ba nk’s required reserves.
(b) Calculate the maximum amount of additional lo ans that Raymond Bank can make without selling its
holdings of government securities.
(c) Assuming that Raymond Bank and ot her banks now lend out all excess reserves, calculate the maximum
possible change in the following.
(i) Demand deposits throughout the banking system
(ii) Total reserves throughout the banking system
(d) Suppose that the country’s central bank purchases $1,000 of Raymond Bank’s holdings of government
securities as part of its open-market operations. Do Raymond Bank’s required reserves initially increase,
decrease, or remain the same as a result of the central bank’s purchase? Explain.

points.description: correctly calculating Raymond Bank’s required reserves, Zero point one zero times twenty thousand dollars equals two thousand dollars.

correctly calculating the maximum amount of loans that Raymond Bank can make, $3,000. Excess Reserves equals Total Reserves minus Required Reserves equals five thousand dollars minus two thousand dollars equals three thousand dollars.

correctly calculating the maximum possible change in demand deposits throughout the banking system, $30,000. Maximum change in deposits equals Excess Reserves times Money Mul t i p l ier equals three thousand dollars times one slash zero point one equals thirty thousand dollars.
stating that the maximum possible change in total reserves throughout the banking system is $0.
stating that Raymond Bank’s required reserves initially will remain the same.
stating that there is no change in demand deposits, OR that only excess reserves increased.

text:
3. Singapore and Japan are trading partners. The Japanese economy is operating at full employment, and Japan’s
current account balance is zero.
(ii)
Japan’s net exports
(a) Assume the exchange rate between the Singapore dollar (SGD) and the Japanese yen (JPY) changes from
1 SGD = 82 JPY to 1 SGD = 65 JPY. Does the JPY appreciate or depreciate against the SGD?
(b) Given your answer in part (a), will each of th e following increase, decrease, or remain the same?
(i) The price of Japanese goods in Singapore dollars
(c) Draw a single correctly labeled graph with the long -run Phillips curve and short-run Phillips curve for Japan.
On the graph, show the new short-run equilibrium as a result of the change in Japan’s net exports from
part (b)(ii), labeled point X.
(d) Given your answer in part (b)(ii), will each of the fo llowing be in deficit, surplus, or zero for Japan?
(i) Current account balance
(ii) Financial (capital) account balance

points.description: stating that J P Y appreciates relative to the S G D.
stating that the price of Japanese goods in Singapore dollars will increase. 

stating that Japan’s net exports will decrease. T he figure shows a graph with a horizontal axis labeled Unemployment Rate, in percent, and a vertical axis labeled Inflation Rate, in percent. There are two lines on the graph. The first line is a downward sloping line labeled S R P C. The line starts near the vertical axis and moves down and to the right, and ends above the horizontal axis. The second line is a vertical line labeled L R P C, and begins on the horizontal, and intersects line S R P C at a point near the middle of each line. There is a point labeled X on the line S R P C, to the right of line L R P C.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the downward-sloping short-run Phillips curve open parenthesis, S R P C, close parenthesis. for Japan. 
showing a vertical long-run Phillips curve open parenthesis, L R P C, close parenthesis. and for showing point X to the right of S R P C.
stating that Japan’s current account balance will be in deficit and that Japan’s financial (capital) account balance will be in surplus.

text:
1. Assume a country's economy is currently in recession.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the long-run aggregate supply, short-run aggregate supply, and aggregate
demand curves, and show each of the following.
(i) Current real output, labeled Y 1, and current price level, labeled PL 1
(ii) Full employment output, labeled Yf
(b)
Identify one action the central bank can take to help the economy recover from the recession.
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the money market, and show the impact of the central bank's action
identified in part (b) on the nominal interest rate.
(d) On your graph for part (a), show the effect of the central bank's action identified in part (b) on real output
and the price level.
( e) Assume there is an increase in business confidence as a result of the central bank's action.
(i) What will happen to the demand for capital goods?
(ii) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market, and show the effect of the change
identified in part (e)(i) on the real interest rate.
(f) Given your answer to part (e), what is the effect on potential real output in the long run? Explain

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph for AD and SRAS, showing PL1 and Y1 at the intersection of AD and SRAS.
drawing a vertical LRAS curve at Yf to the right of Y1.
stating that the central bank can buy bonds, decrease the discount rate, or decrease the required reserve ratio to help the economy recover from the recession.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the money market.
showing a rightward shift in the money supply curve and showing a decrease in the nominal interest rate.
showing a rightward shift in the aggregate demand curve and for showing an increase in real output and price level.
stating that the demand for capital goods increases.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market.
showing a rightward shift in the demand for loanable funds curve and an increase in the real interest rate.
stating that potential real output will increase and for explaining that capital accumulation will increase, causing the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve to shift to the right.

text:
2. The exchange rate between the Australian dollar and the Indian rupee is determined in a flexible foreign
exchange market.
(a) Assume India is currently in recession. What fiscal policy action could the Indian government take to
eliminate the recession?
(b) What would be the effect of the fiscal policy action identified in part (a) on interest rates in India?
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the foreign exchange market for the Australian dollar. Show on your
graph the impact of the change in interest rates identified in part (b) on each of the following.
(i) The supply of Australian dollars
(ii) The equilibrium exchange rate of the Australian dollar
(d) What would be the effect of the change in the exchange rate identified in part (c)(ii) on Australian exports?
( e) What would be the effect of the change in Australian exports identified in part ( d) on Australian
unemployment?

points.description: stating that the government should either increase its expenditures (or purchases) or decrease taxes.
stating that interest rates will increase.
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the foreign exchange market of the Australian dollar.
showing a rightward shift in the supply curve for Australian dollars and a depreciation in the Australian dollar.
stating that Australian exports will increase.
stating that Australian unemployment decreases.

text:
3. Gross domestic product (GDP), unemployment, inflation, and economic growth are key measures of a country's
economic performance.
The following statistics are from the country of Pattiland in 2016; dollar values are measured in 2016 dollars.
(a) Calculate each of the following and show your work.
(i) Nominal GDP
(ii) Real GDP
(iii) Real GDP per capita
(b) Debbie is a citizen of the country of Wayland and owns a computer software firm in Pattiland. Is the output
produced by Debbie's computer software firm included in Pattiland's nominal GDP calculated in part (a)?
Explain.
(c) The GDP deflator for Pattiland in 2015 was 110. In 2016 was Pattiland experiencing inflation or deflation,
or is there insufficient information to determine Pattiland's economic condition? Explain.
( d) Calculate the unemployment rate in Pattiland.

points.description: correctly calculating nominal GDP as $180 ($90+$65+$50+$25–$50).
correctly calculating real GDP as $200 ($180/90 × 100). One point for correctly calculating real GDP per capita as $4 ($200/50).
correctly calculating the GDP deflator as 90 (80/00 x 100).
criteria: correctly calculating the GDP deflator as 90 (80/00 x 100)

points.description: stating that the output produced by Debbie’s computer software firm will be included in Pattiland’s nominal GDP and for explaining that GDP includes all domestically produced final goods and services.

stating that Pattiland is experiencing deflation and for explaining the GDP deflator in 2016 is less than in 2015, or the GDP deflator fell from 110 to 90 between 2015 and 2016 indicating falling prices (deflation).
correctly calculating the unemployment rate as 20% (5/25 × 100).

text:
1. Assume that a country’s economy is currently in recession.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the long-run and short-run Phillips curves. Label the current equilibrium
as point Z.
(b) Assume banks in the country hold no excess reserves and the public’s holding of currency is constant.
The required reserve ratio is 25%. The central bank of the country buys $100 billion in bonds from the
nonbank public.
(i) By how much will the monetary base of the country change?
(ii) Calculate the change in the amount of loans in the banking system in the country.
(iii) Calculate the change in the money supply in the country.
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the money market and show the
effect of the change in the money supply
identified in part (b)(iii) on the nominal interest rate.
(d) Assume there is no change in inflationary expectations. On your graph in part (a), label a point W that is
consistent with the effect of the change in the nominal interest rate identified in part (c).
(e) Based on the change in the interest rate identified in part (c), how will the international value of the
country’s currency change?
(f) Based on your answer in part (e), will the country’s net exports increase, decrease, or stay the same?
Explain

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph of the S R P C .
drawing the L R P C and for showing point Z on the S R P C to the right of the L R P C . b) 3 points:
stating that the monetary base will change by $100 billion.
correctly calculating the change in the amount of loans in the banking system as $300, which is equal to 4 times $75 .
correctly calculating the change in the money supply as $400, which is equal to 4 times $100 .
drawing a correctly labeled graph of the money market.
showing a rightward shift in the money supply curve and a lower nominal inter est rate.
showing point W to the left of point Z on the S R P C .
stating that the country’s currency will depreciate.
stating that net exports will increase and for explaining that this is because the country’s goods will be relatively cheaper than foreign goods.

text:
2. Suppose that the United States government implements a fiscal policy that increases the budget surplus.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market and show the effect of the increase in the
budget surplus on the equilibrium real interest rate.
(b) The European Union is a major trading partner of the United States. Given your answer in part (a) about
the real interest rate, will the United States dollar appreciate or depreciate against the euro? Explain.
(c) Suppose that the Federal Reserve, the central bank of the United States, decides to offset the change in the
value of the dollar identified in part (b).
(i) Would the Federal Reserve buy or sell the euro?
(ii) Would the Federal Re serve buy or sell the dollar?
(d) Suppose that the Federal Reserve wants to counteract the real interest rate change identified in part (a).
What open-market operation would the Federal Reserve use?

points.description: drawing a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market.
showing a leftward shift of the demand curve for loanable funds (or a rightward shift of the supply curve of loanable funds) and a decrease in the equilibrium real interest rate.

stating that the dollar will depreciate against the euro and for explaining that the demand for United States financial assets decreases which decreases the demand for the dollar or increases the supply of the dollar. (It is also acceptable to explain that as investors seek higher returns in foreign financial assets, there will be financial capital outflows, which decreases the demand for the dollar or increases the supply of the dollar.)
stating that the Federal Reserve should sell the euro and buy the dollar.
stating that the Federal Reserve would sell bonds.

text:
3. The table above shows data for the economy of Fruitland, which produces two goods, apples and oranges.
(a) Calculate the nominal gross domestic product
(GDP) for year 2.
(b) Using year 1 as the base year, calculate real GDP for year 2.
(c) Calculate the GDP deflator for year 2.
(d) Assuming the market basket is composed of the quant ities in year 1, calculate the consumer price index
(CPI) for year 2.
(e) Suppose apple and orange pickers received a 2 percent increase in their wages. Based on your answer to
part (d), would real wages of apple and orange pickers increase, decrease, or stay the same from year 1
to year 2 ? Explain. . .

points.description: correctly calculating the nominal G D P for year 2 as 160, which is equal to 6 times 10, plus, 5 times 20 .
correctly calculating the real G D P for year 2 as 240, which is equal to 4 times 10, plus, 10 times 20 .
correctly calculating the G D P deflator for year 2 as 67, which is equal to 160 divided by 240, times 100 .
correctly calculating the C P I for year 2 as 110, which is equal to 110 divided by 100, times 10 .
correctly stating that real wages will decrease and for explaining that the increase in the nominal wage rate is lower than the inflation rate (2% is less than 10%) .

text:
1. Assume that a country's economy is in a short-run equilibrium and the actual unemployment rate is lower than the natural rate of unemployment.
(a) Using a correctly labeled graph of the long-run aggregate supply curve, short-run aggregate supply curve, and aggregate demand curve, show each of the following.
(i) Current price level, labeled PL1, and current output level, labeled Y1.
(ii) The full-employment output level, labeled YF.
(b) Assume the country's banking system has ample reserves. What monetary policy action should the country's central bank use to move the economy toward its long-run equilibrium?
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the country's reserve market, and show how the central bank's action to move the economy toward its long-run equilibrium affects the policy rate in the short run.
(d) Based on the interest rate change from part (c), will each of the following increase, decrease, or remain the same in the short run?
(i) Real output. Explain.
(ii) Natural rate of unemployment.
(e) Assume instead that the central bank does not pursue the monetary policy action from part (b) and there was no other government intervention. Will each of the following increase, decrease, or remain the same in the long run?
(i) Short-run aggregate supply. Explain.
(ii) Employment

points.description: One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph showing a downward-sloping AD curve, an upward-sloping SRAS curve, equilibrium price level PL1, and output level Y1.
One point is earned for showing a vertical LRAS curve at YF to the left of the equilibrium output level.
One point is earned for stating that the central bank should increase its administered interest rates.
One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph of the reserve market with the supply curve intersecting the demand curve in the range of ample reserves.
One point is earned for showing an increase in the administered interest rates, resulting in an increase in the policy rate.
One point is earned for stating that real output will decrease and for explaining that interest-sensitive spending will decrease, resulting in a decrease in aggregate demand and real output.
One point is earned for stating that the natural rate of unemployment will remain unchanged.
One point is earned for stating that the short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve will shift to the left in the long run and for explaining that nominal wages will increase.
One point is earned for stating that employment will decrease.

text:
2. The following is the balance sheet for Raymond Bank, one of many commercial banks in a country in which the banking system has limited reserves.

Assume a 10 percent reserve requirement.
(a) Calculate Raymond Bank's required reserves. Show your work.
(b) Calculate the maximum amount of additional loans that Raymond Bank can make without selling its holdings of government securities. Show your work.
(c) Assume that Raymond Bank and other banks now lend out all excess reserves.
(i) Calculate the maximum possible change in demand deposits throughout the banking system. Show your work.
(ii) What is the maximum possible change in total reserves throughout the banking system?
(d) Suppose that the country's central bank purchases $1,000 of Raymond Bank's holdings of government securities as part of its open-market operations. Do Raymond Bank's required reserves initially increase, decrease, or remain the same as a result of the central bank's purchase? Explain

points.description: One point is earned for correctly calculating Raymond Bank's required reserves, 0.10 x $20,000 = $2,000.
One point is earned for correctly calculating the maximum amount of loans that Raymond Bank can make, $3,000. Excess Reserves = Total Reserves - Required Reserves = $5,000 - $2,000 = $3,000.

One point is earned for correctly calculating the maximum possible change in demand deposits throughout the banking system, $30,000. Maximum change in deposits = Excess Reserves x Money Multiplier = $3,000 x 1/0.1 = $30,000.
One point is earned for stating that the maximum possible change in total reserves throughout the banking system is $0.

One point is earned for stating that Raymond Bank's required reserves initially will remain the same and for explaining that there is no change in demand deposits or that only excess reserves increased.

text:
3. Singapore and Japan are trading partners. The Japanese economy is operating at full employment, and Japan's current account balance is zero.
(a) Assume the exchange rate between the Singapore dollar (SGD) and the Japanese yen (JPY) changes from 1 SGD = 82 JPY to 1 SGD = 65 JPY. Does the JPY appreciate or depreciate against the SGD?
(b) Given your answer in part (a), will each of the following increase, decrease, or remain the same?
(i) The price of Japanese goods in Singapore dollars.
(ii) Japan's net exports.
(c) Draw a single correctly labeled graph with the long-run Phillips curve and short-run Phillips curve for Japan. On the graph, show the new short-run equilibrium as a result of the change in Japan's net exports from part (b)(ii), labeled point X.
(d) Given your answer in part (b)(ii), will each of the following be in deficit, surplus, or zero for Japan?
(i) Current account balance.
(ii) Financial (capital) account balance

points.description: One point is earned for stating that JPY appreciates relative to the SGD.
One point is earned for stating that the price of Japanese goods in Singapore dollars will increase.
One point is earned for stating that Japan's net exports will decrease.
One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph of the downward-sloping short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) for Japan.
One point is earned for showing a vertical long-run Phillips curve (LRPC) and for showing point X to the right of the LRPC on the SRPC.
One point is earned for stating that Japan's current account balance will be in deficit and that Japan's financial (capital) account balance will be in surplus.

text:
1. Assume a country's economy is currently in recession.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the long-run aggregate supply, short-run aggregate supply, and aggregate demand curves, and show each of the following.
(i) Current real output, labeled Y1, and current price level, labeled PL1.
(ii) Full employment output, labeled YF.
(b) Assuming the banking system has ample reserves, identify one action the central bank can take to help the economy recover from the recession.
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the reserve market, and show the impact of the central bank's action identified in part (b) on the policy rate.
(d) On your graph for part (a), show the effect of the central bank's action identified in part (b) on real output and the price level.
(e) Assume there is an increase in business confidence as a result of the central bank's action.
(i) What will happen to the demand for capital goods?
(ii) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market, and show the effect of the change identified in part (e)(i) on the real interest rate.
(f) Given your answer to part (e), what is the effect on potential real output in the long run? Explain

points.description: One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph for AD and SRAS, showing PL1 and Y1 at the intersection of AD and SRAS.
One point is earned for drawing a vertical LRAS curve at YF to the right of Y1.
One point is earned for stating that the central bank can decrease its administered interest rates (e.g., interest on reserves) to help the economy recover from the recession.
One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph of the reserve market with the supply curve intersecting the demand curve in the range of ample reserves.
One point is earned for showing a decrease in the administered interest rates, resulting in a decrease in the policy rate.

One point is earned for showing a rightward shift in the aggregate demand curve and for showing an increase in real output and price level. The rightward shift in AD does not have to eliminate the output gap.
One point is earned for stating that the demand for capital goods increases.
One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market.
One point is earned for showing a rightward shift in the demand for loanable funds curve and an increase in the real interest rate.

One point is earned for stating that potential real output will increase and for explaining that capital accumulation will increase, causing the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve to shift to the right.

text:
2. The exchange rate between the Australian dollar and the Indian rupee is determined in a flexible foreign exchange market.
(a) Assume India is currently in recession. What fiscal policy action could the Indian government take to eliminate the recession?
(b) What would be the effect of the fiscal policy action identified in part (a) on interest rates in India?
(c) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the foreign exchange market for the Australian dollar. Show on your graph the impact of the change in interest rates identified in part (b) on each of the following.
(i) The supply of Australian dollars.
(ii) The equilibrium exchange rate of the Australian dollar.
(d) What would be the effect of the change in the exchange rate identified in part (c)(ii) on Australian exports?
(e) What would be the effect of the change in Australian exports identified in part (d) on Australian unemployment?

points.description: One point is earned for stating that the Indian government should increase government spending or decrease taxes.
One point is earned for stating that the interest rate in India will increase (or for explaining that the demand for loanable funds will increase or the supply of loanable funds will decrease).
One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph of the foreign exchange market for the Australian dollar.
One point is earned for showing a leftward shift in the supply of Australian dollars (or a rightward shift in the demand for Australian dollars) and an appreciation of the Australian dollar.
One point is earned for stating that Australian exports will decrease.
One point is earned for stating that Australian unemployment will increase.

text:
3. Gross domestic product (GDP), unemployment, inflation, and economic growth are key measures of a country's economic performance.
The following statistics are from the country of Pattiland in 2016; dollar values are measured in 2016 dollars.

(a) Calculate each of the following and show your work.
(i) Nominal GDP.
(ii) Real GDP.
(iii) Real GDP per capita.
(b) Debbie is a citizen of the country of Wayland and owns a computer software firm in Pattiland. Is the output produced by Debbie's computer software firm included in Pattiland's nominal GDP calculated in part (a)? Explain.
(c) The GDP deflator for Pattiland in 2015 was 110. In 2016 was Pattiland experiencing inflation or deflation, or is there insufficient information to determine Pattiland's economic condition? Explain.
(d) Calculate the unemployment rate in Pattiland. Show your work

points.description: One point is earned for correctly calculating nominal GDP.
One point is earned for correctly calculating real GDP.
One point is earned for correctly calculating real GDP per capita.
One point is earned for stating that the output is included in Pattiland's nominal GDP and for explaining that the firm is located in Pattiland (or that GDP is based on the location of production).
One point is earned for stating that there is insufficient information and for explaining that the GDP deflator must be compared to the previous year's deflator (or that the base year is needed).
One point is earned for correctly calculating the unemployment rate.

text:
1. Assume that a country's economy is currently in recession.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the long-run and short-run Phillips curves. Label the current equilibrium as point Z.
(b) Assume the banking system has limited reserves, banks in the country hold no excess reserves, and the public's holding of currency is constant. The required reserve ratio is 25%. The central bank of the country buys $100 billion in bonds from the nonbank public.

(d) Assume there is no change in inflationary expectations. On your graph in part (a), label a point W that is consistent with the effect of the change in the nominal interest rate identified in part (c).
(e) Based on the change in the interest rate identified in part (c), how will the international value of the country's currency change?
(f) Based on your answer in part (e), will the country's net exports increase, decrease, or stay the same? Explain

points.description: One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph of the SRPC.
One point is earned for drawing the LRPC and for showing point Z on the SRPC to the right of the LRPC.
One point is earned for stating that the monetary base will change by $100 billion.

One point is earned for correctly calculating the change in the money supply as $400 (=4x$100).

One point is earned for showing point W to the left of point Z on the SRPC.

text:
2. Suppose that the United States government implements a fiscal policy that increases the budget surplus.
(a) Draw a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market and show the effect of the increase in the budget surplus on the equilibrium real interest rate.
(b) The European Union is a major trading partner of the United States. Given your answer in part (a) about the real interest rate, will the United States dollar appreciate or depreciate against the euro? Explain.
(c) Suppose that the Federal Reserve, the central bank of the United States, decides to offset the change in the value of the dollar identified in part (b).
(i) Would the Federal Reserve buy or sell the euro?
(ii) Would the Federal Reserve buy or sell the dollar?
(d) Suppose that the Federal Reserve wants to counteract the real interest rate change identified in part (a). What monetary policy action would the Federal Reserve take?

points.description: One point is earned for drawing a correctly labeled graph of the loanable funds market and showing a leftward shift of the demand curve (or rightward shift of supply) and a decrease in the equilibrium real interest rate.
One point is earned for stating that the dollar will depreciate against the euro and for explaining that the demand for United States financial assets decreases.
One point is earned for stating that the Federal Reserve should sell the euro and buy the dollar.
One point is earned for stating that the Federal Reserve should buy the dollar (and sell the euro).
One point is earned for stating that the Federal Reserve would increase its administered interest rates (e.g., interest on reserves).

text:
3. The table above shows data for the economy of Fruitland, which produces two goods, apples and oranges.
(a) Calculate the nominal gross domestic product (GDP) for year 2. Show your work.
(b) Using year 1 as the base year, calculate real GDP for year 2. Show your work.
(c) Calculate the GDP deflator for year 2. Show your work.
(d) Assuming the market basket is composed of the quantities in year 1, calculate the consumer price index (CPI) for year 2. Show your work.
(e) Suppose apple and orange pickers received a 2 percent increase in their wages. Based on your answer to part (d), would real wages of apple and orange pickers increase, decrease, or stay the same from year 1 to year 2? Explain

points.description: One point is earned for correctly calculating the nominal GDP for year 2 as 160 (=6x10+5x20).
One point is earned for correctly calculating the real GDP for year 2 as 240 (=4x10+10x20).
One point is earned for correctly calculating the GDP deflator for year 2 as 67 (=160/240 x100).
One point is earned for correctly calculating the CPI for year 2 as 110 (=110/100 x100).
One point is earned for correctly stating that real wages will decrease and for explaining that the increase in the nominal wage rate is lower than the inflation rate (2% < 10%).
